entries.definition:
كتاب الواو

وصم - وصي

(وصي) الواو والصاد والحرف المعتلّ: أصلٌ يدلُّ على وَصلِ شيءٍ بشيء. ووَصَيْتُ الشَّيءَ: وصَلْتُه. ويقال: وطِئْنا أرضاً واصيةً، أي إنَّ نَبتَها متَّصلٌ قد امتلأَتْ منه. ووَصَيْتُ اللّيلَةَ باليوم: وصَلْتُها، وذلك في عملٍ تَعمَلُه. والوصِيَّة من هذا القياس، كأنّه كلامٌ يُوصَى أي يُوصَل. يقال: وصَّيْتُه توصيةً، وأوصَيْتُه إيصاء.

وصب - وصد - وصر - وضع
entries.definition_english:
Here is the translation of the dictionary entry for the root "و ص ي" from Ibn Faris's *Maqayis al-Lughah*:

### Core Semantic Principles
The letters *wāw*, *ṣād*, and the weak letter (here *yāʾ*) constitute a fundamental principle. This principle signifies the **connection of one thing to another**.

### Derivations & Evidence
Ibn Faris illustrates this core principle through various linguistic derivations and examples:

*   **Direct Connection**: The most direct application is seen in the phrase *waṣaytu al-shayʾa*, meaning "I connected the thing" (*waṣaltuhu*). This confirms the root's foundational meaning of establishing a link.

*   **Physical Connection**: The principle extends to physical descriptions. It is said, "We trod upon a *wāṣiya* land." Here, *wāṣiya* describes land whose vegetation is densely **connected** and has completely filled it, indicating a continuous and pervasive growth. This exemplifies the physical manifestation of the root's core meaning.

*   **Temporal Connection**: The root can also denote temporal continuity. For instance, the expression *waṣaytu al-laylata bi'l-yawm* means "I **connected** the night with the day." Ibn Faris explains this in the context of an action or task that is carried out without interruption, thus linking one period of time to the next.

*   **The Concept of *al-Waṣiyya* (Bequest or Injunction)**:
    *   Ibn Faris explicitly links the term *al-waṣiyya* (meaning a will, bequest, or injunction) to this core semantic principle.
    *   He explains that *al-waṣiyya* is "as if it were speech that is *yūṣā* (enjoined or bequeathed), meaning it is *yūṣal* (connected)."
    *   This indicates that a *waṣiyya* acts as a **connection** between the person making the will or giving the injunction and the recipient. It establishes a link of command, guidance, or transfer of property that extends into the future, often beyond the presence or even life of the speaker. The words or intentions are "connected" to the actions or responsibilities of another.
    *   Further verbal forms expressing this act are *waṣṣaytu-hu tawṣiyatan* and *awṣaytu-hu īṣāʾan*, both signifying the act of entrusting, enjoining, or bequeathing, all fundamentally stemming from the idea of establishing a connection of responsibility or instruction.

entries.definition:
w–ṣ–y thick, intertwined plants; a will, instruction; to
bequeath, to urge; to impress upon someone, to relate to someone;
to obligate; to join together. Of this root, six forms occur 32 times
in the Qur’an: وَصَّى waṣṣā 11 times; أَوْصَى ʾawṣā six times; تَوَاصَى
tawāṣā five times; مُوصٍ mūṣī once; وَصِيَّة waṣiyyatun eight times
and تَوْصِيَة tawṣiyatun once.

وَصَّى waṣṣā [v. II, trans.] to enjoin upon, to prescribe, to
command; to leave to, to bequeath to (2:132) وَوَصَّى بِهَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ بَنِيهِ
and Abraham enjoined it upon his sons.

أَوْصَى ʾawṣā [v. IV, trans.] 1 to enjoin, to command (19:31)
وَأَوْصَانِي بِالصَّلَاةِ وَالزَّكَاةِ and He has enjoined upon me praying and
alms-giving 2 to bequeath (4:12) مِنْ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ تُوصُونَ بِهَا أَوْ دَيْنٍ after
[payment of any other] bequest he may bequeath or [any] debt.

تَوَاصَى tawāṣā [v. VI, intrans.] to urge each other (to do
something) (90:17) وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالْمَرْحَمَةِ and [be of those
who] enjoin upon one another steadfastness and enjoin upon one
another compassion.

1030
